devices.name: Antithesis
devices.definition: A rhetorical figure that places two contrasting ideas in parallel grammatical structure, sharpening each by proximity to its opposite.

detectionSignals.description: Detects two semantically opposed spans linked by parallel structure.
Confirms the opposed clauses share matching grammatical shape.

purposes.description: Sharpen opposition between two ideas
Create rhetorical balance through parallel structure

examples.text: It was the best of times, it was the worst of times.
examples.source: Charles Dickens, A Tale of Two Cities (1859)

devices.name: Juxtaposition
devices.definition: The placement of two dissimilar or contrasting elements side by side without a grammatical connective, letting the reader infer the relationship between them.

detectionSignals.description: Flags two incongruous images or ideas placed within a shared local window.
Confirms the contrasting elements occupy adjacent lines or clauses.

purposes.description: Highlight difference by proximity rather than statement
Unsettle expectation by pairing unlike images

examples.text: A dog starv'd at his Master's Gate, / Predicts the ruin of the State.

devices.name: Paradox
devices.definition: A statement that appears self-contradictory or logically impossible on its surface, yet reveals a deeper coherent truth on reflection.

detectionSignals.description: Detects a single clause asserting mutually exclusive predicates of the same subject.
Confirms the contradiction resolves to a coherent reading rather than nonsense.

purposes.description: Force reflection to resolve an apparent contradiction
Hold two truths in one compact statement

examples.text: I must be cruel, only to be kind.

devices.name: Oxymoron
devices.definition: A compressed figure combining two normally incompatible terms, usually adjective and noun, into a single tightly bound phrase.

detectionSignals.description: Detects two contradictory terms within a tight token span, typically adjective-noun.
Flags idiomatic compressed-contradiction phrases not caught by the adjective-noun pattern.

purposes.description: Compress contradiction into a single vivid phrase
Say two opposed things in minimal space

examples.source: William Shakespeare, Romeo and Juliet, Act 2 Scene 2 (c. 1597)

devices.name: Metaphor
devices.definition: A figure that asserts one thing is another, mapping the qualities of a source domain onto a target domain without an explicit comparison marker.

detectionSignals.description: Detects an equative or identity construction between two domains without 'like'/'as'.
Flags cross-domain attribute mapping consistent with conceptual metaphor.

purposes.description: Map qualities from one domain onto another
Make an abstraction concrete through imagery
State a comparison as identity for economy

examples.text: All the world's a stage, and all the men and women merely players.
examples.source: William Shakespeare, As You Like It, Act 2 Scene 7 (c. 1599)

devices.name: Simile
devices.definition: A figure that compares two unlike things using an explicit comparison marker such as 'like' or 'as', naming both the compared item and its analogue.

detectionSignals.description: Detects an explicit comparison marker joining two distinct referents.

purposes.description: Compare two things explicitly
Make an image concrete through analogy
Signal the comparison openly rather than by assertion

examples.text: O my Luve's like a red, red rose / That's newly sprung in June.
examples.source: Robert Burns, A Red, Red Rose (1794)

devices.name: Anaphora
devices.definition: The deliberate repetition of a word or phrase at the beginning of successive clauses, lines, or sentences to build emphasis and momentum.

detectionSignals.description: Detects the same token or phrase recurring at clause- or line-initial position.
Confirms the repeated span anchors the start of each unit.

purposes.description: Drive emphasis through repeated openings
Build rhetorical momentum across successive units
Bind a passage together around a repeated phrase

examples.text: Blessed are they that mourn: for they shall be comforted. Blessed are the meek: for they shall inherit the earth.

devices.name: Volta
devices.definition: A structural turn, typically in a sonnet, where the argument, tone, or emotional register pivots, most often between octave and sestet or before a closing couplet.

detectionSignals.description: Flags a structural pivot near the conventional turn position (e.g. line 9 or the closing couplet).
Confirms a tonal or argumentative reversal across the pivot.

purposes.description: Pivot the argument or tone
Resolve tension built in the preceding lines
Release accumulated pressure at the structural turn

examples.text: But thy eternal summer shall not fade
examples.note: Marks the turn from the beloved's mortality to art's permanence.

devices.name: Refrain
devices.definition: A line or group of lines repeated at intervals, often at the end of stanzas, that anchors a poem or song around a recurring idea or sound.

detectionSignals.description: Detects an identical or near-identical span recurring at stanza boundaries.
Confirms the recurring span lands at the same structural position each time.

purposes.description: Anchor the piece around a recurring idea
Reinforce a phrase through repetition
Create rhythmic and sonic return

devices.name: Personification
devices.definition: A figure that attributes human qualities, actions, or agency to a nonhuman entity, object, or abstraction.

detectionSignals.description: Flags a human attribute or action predicated of a nonhuman subject.
Flags an agency verb (act, think, choose) governed by an inanimate subject.

purposes.description: Make the nonhuman vivid through human behavior
Project felt emotion onto the world
Grant agency to what has none

examples.text: The fog comes on little cat feet.
